title: Seizures and Status Epilepticus
objectives: Classify seizures as focal vs generalized
Recognize status epilepticus as a medical emergency
Apply the stepped treatment protocol for status epilepticus
Identify common seizure etiologies and mimics
Manage post-ictal state and determine disposition

vignette.text:
A 45-year-old man is brought to the ED by ambulance after a witnessed seizure at work. Coworkers report he suddenly cried out, fell to the ground, and had rhythmic jerking of all extremities lasting approximately 2 minutes. He was confused afterward and is now becoming more alert. He has no prior seizure history but had a head injury in a car accident 3 months ago.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, Traumatic brain injury 3 months ago (brief hospitalization, CT showed small frontal contusion), Depression

Medications: Lisinopril 10mg daily, Bupropion 300mg daily

Social History: Construction worker, drinks 6-8 beers daily, quit smoking 2 years ago

Allergies: None

physicalExam.general: Middle-aged man, drowsy but arousable, appears post-ictal
physicalExam.HEENT: Lateral tongue laceration (bite mark), no hemotympanum
physicalExam.extremities: Abrasion on right elbow from fall

neurologic.mental_status: GCS 14 (E4V4M6), oriented to person and place, not time; following commands
neurologic.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, no facial asymmetry
neurologic.motor: Moving all extremities equally, mild generalized weakness
neurologic.post_ictal: Confused, fatigued, gradual improvement over exam

imaging.CT_head: Encephalomalacia in right frontal lobe consistent with prior contusion. No acute hemorrhage, mass, or midline shift.

questions.stem: His CT shows right frontal encephalomalacia from prior TBI.
questions.question: How does structural brain injury lead to seizures?
questions.hint: Damaged brain tissue creates abnormal electrical activity.

options.text: Gliosis and scarring create epileptogenic foci with abnormal neuronal firing
Seizures only occur at time of injury

questions.explanation: Post-traumatic epilepsy occurs in 5-50% of TBI patients depending on severity. Damaged cortex forms gliotic scars that become epileptogenic foci due to altered neuronal connectivity, ion channel changes, and imbalanced excitation/inhibition. Risk persists for years.

questions.stem: He had a generalized tonic-clonic seizure.
questions.question: What brain structures are involved in a generalized tonic-clonic seizure?
questions.hint: Generalized means both hemispheres from onset.

options.text: Both cerebral hemispheres simultaneously, with spread to brainstem reticular formation

questions.explanation: Generalized tonic-clonic seizures involve both hemispheres from the outset. The tonic phase involves sustained contraction (reticular formation, motor cortex). The clonic phase is rhythmic jerking from oscillating excitation/inhibition. Loss of consciousness reflects bilateral cortical involvement.

questions.stem: He has a lateral tongue laceration.
questions.question: Why does tongue biting occur during seizures, and what does its location suggest?
questions.hint: Consider the mechanism of jaw clenching.

options.text: Tonic jaw contraction during seizure; LATERAL tongue bite is specific for epileptic seizure vs psychogenic
Only tip of tongue is bitten
Tongue biting doesn't occur in true seizures

questions.explanation: Lateral tongue laceration from masseter contraction during tonic phase is highly specific for epileptic seizure (vs psychogenic non-epileptic seizure, where tip biting may occur). Tongue biting occurs in ~25% of GTCs and is valuable historical evidence of true seizure.

questions.stem: His lactate is elevated at 3.8 mmol/L.
questions.question: Why is lactate elevated after a generalized seizure?
questions.hint: Consider the intense muscle activity.

options.text: Massive muscle contraction causes anaerobic metabolism and lactate production
Lactate is not affected by seizures

questions.explanation: Generalized tonic-clonic seizures cause intense, sustained muscle contraction. Muscles exceed aerobic capacity, switching to anaerobic glycolysis and producing lactate. This typically normalizes within 1-2 hours post-ictally. Persistent elevation suggests ongoing seizure or other etiology.

questions.stem: His prolactin is elevated at 42 ng/mL.
questions.question: What is the significance of elevated prolactin after seizure?
questions.hint: Prolactin rises after certain types of seizures.

options.text: Prolactin has no role in seizure evaluation
Prolactin elevates 10-20 minutes after GTC or complex partial seizures; helps distinguish from psychogenic

questions.explanation: Serum prolactin rises 2-3x baseline within 10-20 minutes after GTC or temporal lobe seizures, returning to normal within 2-6 hours. This helps differentiate epileptic seizures from psychogenic events (no prolactin rise). Should be drawn within 20-30 min of event. Frontal seizures may not elevate prolactin.

questions.stem: His sodium is 128 mEq/L.
questions.question: How does hyponatremia cause seizures?
questions.hint: Consider neuronal excitability and cerebral edema.

options.text: Hyponatremia causes water to shift into neurons (cerebral edema), lowering seizure threshold

questions.explanation: Hyponatremia creates osmotic gradient driving water into brain cells, causing cerebral edema and lowering seizure threshold. Seizures typically occur when Na <120 mEq/L or with rapid drops. His hyponatremia (128) likely contributed to his seizure, especially combined with other risk factors.

questions.stem: He is in the post-ictal state.
questions.question: What causes the post-ictal state?
questions.hint: The brain needs time to recover from seizure activity.

options.text: Neuronal exhaustion, depleted energy stores, increased inhibitory tone, and transient dysfunction

questions.explanation: Post-ictal state results from: neuronal exhaustion (depleted ATP, glucose), active inhibition to terminate seizure (increased GABA), transient blood-brain barrier changes, and metabolic derangements. Duration varies from minutes to hours. Prolonged post-ictal state suggests status or underlying lesion.

questions.stem: He has multiple potential seizure etiologies.
questions.question: Which factors likely contributed to his first seizure?
questions.hint: Multiple hits can lower seizure threshold.

options.text: Post-traumatic epilepsy (frontal lesion) + hyponatremia + alcohol withdrawal + bupropion (lowers threshold)

questions.explanation: He has multiple seizure risk factors: (1) Post-traumatic epileptogenic focus (frontal contusion); (2) Hyponatremia (128); (3) Heavy alcohol use → possible withdrawal; (4) Bupropion (dose-dependent seizure risk, especially at 300mg); (5) Low magnesium. Multiple factors combine to lower threshold.

questions.stem: He drinks 6-8 beers daily.
questions.question: How does alcohol withdrawal cause seizures?
questions.hint: Consider the effect of chronic alcohol on GABA receptors.

options.text: Alcohol directly causes seizures while drinking
Chronic alcohol enhances GABA; withdrawal causes GABA receptor downregulation and glutamate upregulation → hyperexcitability
Only occurs with decades of alcohol use

questions.explanation: Alcohol potentiates GABA inhibition and inhibits NMDA glutamate receptors. Chronic use → receptor adaptation. Withdrawal removes alcohol's inhibitory effect on a nervous system that has compensatorily reduced inhibition and enhanced excitation → seizures, typically 12-48 hours after last drink.

questions.stem: He takes bupropion 300mg daily.
questions.question: Why is bupropion associated with seizure risk?
questions.hint: Consider its mechanism and dose relationship.

options.text: Bupropion lowers seizure threshold in dose-dependent manner; risk increases above 300mg/day

questions.explanation: Bupropion is a norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitor that lowers seizure threshold, especially at doses >300mg/day (0.4% risk at therapeutic doses). Risk increases with rapid titration, eating disorders, alcohol withdrawal, or other pro-convulsant factors. His dose + other risks increased his seizure risk.

questions.stem: He had a single seizure. You're considering whether to start an AED.
questions.question: What is the indication for starting an antiepileptic drug after a first seizure?
questions.hint: Risk of recurrence guides treatment.

options.text: Start AED if high recurrence risk: abnormal EEG, structural lesion, or epileptogenic etiology
All first seizures require lifelong AED
Only start if second seizure occurs within 24 hours
Never start AED after first seizure

questions.explanation: After a first unprovoked seizure, recurrence risk is ~40% at 2 years. High-risk features (abnormal EEG, structural brain abnormality, nocturnal seizure, status epilepticus) increase this to >60%. AEDs reduce recurrence but don't affect long-term remission. His frontal lesion = high risk → AED indicated.

questions.stem: You decide to start an AED.
questions.question: Which AED is a good first-line choice for focal seizures with secondary generalization?
questions.hint: Consider efficacy and tolerability.

options.text: Levetiracetam (Keppra) - broad spectrum, no titration needed, few interactions

questions.explanation: Levetiracetam is often first-line: broad spectrum (focal and generalized), no titration required, few drug interactions, no hepatic metabolism issues. Alternatives: lamotrigine (needs slow titration), lacosamide, valproate. Phenytoin effective but more side effects and interactions. Carbamazepine induces enzymes.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for status epilepticus?
questions.hint: Benzodiazepines are first line.

options.text: IV lorazepam 4mg (or IM midazolam if no IV access)

questions.explanation: First-line: IV lorazepam 4mg (0.1 mg/kg) or IM midazolam 10mg (if no IV). Benzodiazepines enhance GABA inhibition and stop 60-80% of status epilepticus. questions.stem: Given his alcohol use, benzodiazepines serve dual purpose.
questions.question: How do benzodiazepines help with alcohol withdrawal seizure prevention?
questions.hint: Consider the mechanism of alcohol withdrawal.

options.text: Benzodiazepines are contraindicated in alcohol withdrawal
Only phenytoin works for alcohol withdrawal seizures
Benzodiazepines cross-react with GABA receptors, substituting for alcohol's inhibitory effect

questions.explanation: Benzodiazepines are first-line for alcohol withdrawal because they potentiate GABA-A receptors similarly to alcohol. They prevent withdrawal seizures and treat the hyperadrenergic state. Phenytoin does NOT prevent alcohol withdrawal seizures (different mechanism). Use symptom-triggered benzodiazepine protocol (CIWA).

questions.stem: You need to classify his seizure.
questions.question: How is a generalized tonic-clonic seizure classified?
questions.hint: Use ILAE classification criteria.

options.text: Generalized onset motor seizure (tonic-clonic) - or focal to bilateral tonic-clonic if focal features preceded

questions.explanation: ILAE 2017 classification: Generalized onset (both hemispheres from start) vs Focal onset (one hemisphere initially). His seizure could be generalized onset tonic-clonic OR focal to bilateral tonic-clonic (if started focally from frontal lesion and spread). EEG helps distinguish. Witness history of focal onset signs is key.

questions.stem: You need to determine the workup.
questions.question: What is the essential workup for a first-time seizure?
questions.hint: Rule out provoking factors and structural causes.

options.text: Labs (glucose, Na, Ca, Mg, toxicology), CT head, consider EEG and MRI
No workup needed for first seizure

questions.explanation: First seizure workup: Labs (glucose, electrolytes including Na/Ca/Mg, toxicology, consider prolactin), CT head (emergent to rule out hemorrhage/mass), EEG (can be outpatient if stable), MRI (more sensitive for epileptogenic lesions). LP if infection suspected. He has known structural lesion (TBI).

questions.stem: You're differentiating seizure from syncope.
questions.question: What features distinguish seizure from syncope?
questions.hint: The history provides critical clues.

options.text: Syncope never has any jerking movements
Seizure: lateral tongue bite, post-ictal confusion >5 min, tonic-clonic activity >30 sec; Syncope: brief LOC, rapid recovery, pallor

questions.explanation: Seizure features: lateral tongue bite (most specific), prolonged post-ictal confusion (>5 min), witnessed tonic-clonic activity (>30 sec), head turning, cyanosis. Syncope: prodrome (lightheadedness, warmth), brief LOC with rapid recovery (<1 min), pallor. Convulsive syncope has brief myoclonic jerks but not sustained tonic-clonic activity.

questions.stem: He asks about driving.
questions.question: What are typical driving restrictions after a seizure?
questions.hint: Regulations vary by state but have common principles.

options.text: Typically 3-12 months seizure-free, depending on state laws and seizure type
Can drive immediately after treatment starts

questions.explanation: Most states require 3-12 months seizure-free before driving. Some states mandate physician reporting; others require patient self-report. Factors considered: seizure type, AED compliance, time of day. Commercial driving restrictions are stricter (usually seizure-free without AEDs). Document counseling.

questions.stem: If his seizure had continued for >5 minutes.
questions.question: What defines status epilepticus and why is it dangerous?
questions.hint: Prolonged seizure causes brain injury.

options.text: Only absence seizures can become status
Seizure ≥5 minutes or recurrent seizures without recovery; causes excitotoxic brain injury, hypoxia, acidosis

questions.explanation: Status epilepticus: continuous seizure ≥5 minutes or ≥2 seizures without full recovery. Beyond 30 minutes, excitotoxic neuronal injury occurs (NMDA receptor activation, calcium influx, cell death). Also causes systemic complications: hypoxia, acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, hyperthermia, cardiac arrhythmias. Mortality 20%+ if prolonged.

questions.stem: You're concerned about sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP).
questions.question: What is SUDEP and what are risk factors?
questions.hint: This is the most common cause of death in epilepsy.

options.text: Sudden death in epilepsy patients without identified cause; risk factors: uncontrolled GTCs, non-adherence, nocturnal seizures
SUDEP is extremely rare and not worth discussing
All epilepsy patients have same SUDEP risk

questions.explanation: SUDEP: sudden unexpected death in epilepsy, likely from seizure-induced respiratory/cardiac dysfunction. Incidence: 1/1000 patient-years overall, higher in refractory epilepsy. Risk factors: frequent GTCs, non-adherence, nocturnal seizures, young age, sleeping prone. AED adherence reduces risk. Discuss with patients.

questions.stem: Planning comprehensive management.
questions.question: What is the comprehensive management plan?

options.text: Start AED, correct hyponatremia, alcohol withdrawal precautions, reconsider bupropion, outpatient EEG/MRI, driving counseling, neurology follow-up
ICU admission for all first seizures

questions.explanation: Comprehensive plan: (1) Start levetiracetam given high recurrence risk (structural lesion); (2) Correct hyponatremia slowly; (3) Alcohol withdrawal protocol (CIWA, thiamine); (4) Discuss bupropion alternatives with psychiatry; (5) Outpatient EEG, consider MRI; (6) Driving restriction counseling; (7) Neurology follow-up; (8) SUDEP counseling.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected seizure?

options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in seizure. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of seizure. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.

mcqs.stem: When managing seizure, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
